scope: file (multi-file integration / E2E / migration / platform-matrix test layer)
detailed_analysis: This 'integration / E2E / migration / platform-matrix' test layer is — with one important exception — not what its directory naming claims to be. The single shared syncer fixture in conftest.py is what every E2E test in this scope actually depends on, and it constructs a Syncer over a tmp_path with no daemon loop, no real OS user dirs, no subprocesses, and no network. Calls to syncer.sync_once() exercise the in-process sync algorithm against real files on a real (temp) filesystem, which is the strongest part of this layer: tests in test_e2e_sync.py, test_first_boot_reconciliation.py, and test_v0_4_1_matrix.py genuinely drive the production code path end-to-end. The problem layer is the platform-matrix and migration files, which are mislabeled. (1) test_macos_compat.py and test_windows_slug_collision.py are NOT macOS / Windows tests — they are pure Linux tests that monkeypatch sys.platform and os.path.normcase to simulate the other OS. They will run (and pass) on Linux CI exactly as they will on darwin/win32 because nothing in them actually depends on the host filesystem's case-folding or NFD/NFC behavior. (2) test_windows_silent_startup.py is a static text test on install.ps1: it asserts that hard-coded substrings appear in the PowerShell source. This is grep-as-test. (3) test_windows_filesystem_retries.py monkeypatches time.sleep to a no-op and stubs the retry callable. (4) test_migrate_v0_4.py is the most misleading file. The migration script targets a real on-disk v0.3 layout, but every test either tests _run_phase with raise RuntimeError('simulated') or monkeypatches stop_daemon and _snapshot_tool_roots so the migration is replaced with stubs. Not one test materializes a real pre-fix layout in tmp_path, runs the real run_migration, and asserts the post-migration filesystem state. (5) test_state_schema_v3 calls time.sleep(0.01) and then asserts second > first — flakiness vector. (6) conftest.syncer is a god-fixture: a single 35-line fixture creates 13 subdirectories and configures 4 agentic tools for every test. (7) None of the E2E tests are marked slow or integration. (8) test_e2e_sync.test_unreadable_prior_text_logs_warning_and_continues is the most fragile test in the scope: it asserts that a WARNING containing the string 'Could not read prior text' appears in caplog.

findings.problem: File is named test_macos_compat.py and tests are named 'test_darwin_...' / 'test_linux_...', implying platform-conditional coverage. In practice every test runs on Linux CI: sys.platform is monkeypatched, the temp filesystem is whatever the host provides, and no test is gated by @pytest.mark.skipif. A real macOS-specific filesystem regression would not be caught.

The 'windows silent startup' test never starts anything: it loads install.ps1 as text and grep-asserts substrings. A PowerShell syntax error, a quoting regression, or a wscript launch that opens a visible window would all silently pass.

The migration script's actual purpose — detect a real v0.3 on-disk layout and re-baseline it — is not exercised by any test. Test 1 calls _run_phase with a boom() lambda. Test 2 asserts the trivial pass-through. Test 3 monkeypatches stop_daemon and _snapshot_tool_roots so the migration becomes its own stub. Migration-script bugs are exactly the kind of one-shot, never-rerun failure that test_e2e_sync.py cannot catch.

A single fixture wires four tools, 13 directories, and 4 config-file paths for every consumer — including tests that conceptually exercise a single tool pair. Two-letter directory names ('as', 'or', 'oc') make the wiring even harder to read.
This test rebuilds the conftest syncer fixture inline (33 lines) because it needs antigravity_enabled=False. The shared fixture lacks parametrization.
time.sleep(0.01) to force last_modified to advance is a wall-clock dependency. On a system whose time.time() resolution is coarser than 10ms, the assert fails non-deterministically.

findings.variant: Fragile Test / Coupling to Log Message Text
findings.problem: The assertion encodes the contract as a literal substring search ('Could not read prior text') against the human-readable log message and over the root logger. Any rewording breaks the test without changing behavior.
Single test verifies both (a) warning emission and (b) successful fallback to prior_text=None and continued propagation. Two distinct behaviors share one Arrange/Act.

The test asserts the wrong behavior on purpose — block-and-log when the spec calls for managed-wins-and-archive — as a tripwire for when the deferred §5.5 handler is implemented. The right shape is @pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True, reason='§5.5 deferred').
Nine independent assertions verify four distinct behaviors.

findings.variant: Mislabeled Platform Test (no real Windows behavior exercised)
findings.problem: File name promises Windows filesystem coverage. In practice the test injects a flaky() callable that raises PermissionError on count<3 and time.sleep is stubbed to no-op.
Same pattern as test_macos_compat.py: file names a platform but body monkeypatches os.path.normcase to simulate it.
Three tests in this file each write a SKILL.md + .DS_Store + ._asset.png trio with identical content.
_skill_md is defined in both test_e2e_sync.py and test_first_boot_reconciliation.py; _set_mtime / _set_skill_mtime appear in both.
Tests that build a 13-directory tmp tree, instantiate a 4-tool Syncer, and run sync_once() are unmarked. A developer running pytest tests/ cannot opt out of the integration layer.
Test verifies file content and that codex side is empty, but does not assert that state.json records nothing for this duplicate pair_id.

verdict_summary: E2E tests against Syncer.sync_once are genuine and the strongest part of the scope, but the migration and platform-matrix files overpromise: 'macOS' and 'Windows' tests run on Linux via monkeypatch, the install.ps1 'silent startup' test is grep-as-test, and the v0.4 migration script's actual job is never exercised against a real pre-fix layout.
